and you can see on the left-hand side after the fourth sternotomy and the right pulmonary artery is visualized.
In the middle picture, the hilar branches are all dissected with vessel loops.
Externally, you may not notice that the right pulmonary artery has any significant abnormality, however,
On the right-hand picture, you can see clearly the transected right pulmonary artery with a nearly 1 mm internal diameter of the patent segment of the right pulmonary artery.

This is the view after the reconstruction with the Gore-Tex graft, which is nicely positioned, and 12 mm is a good size for this infant that matches both the proximal and distal pulmonary artery and branches.
A fourth time sternotomy was performed and the chest was entered without difficulty.
We continued our mediastinal dissection exposing the free wall of the right atrium and the superior vena cava was mobilized paying attention to the right phrenic nerve.
This is important to facilitate exposure of the hilar branches of the right pulmonary artery.
Right pulmonary artery is being mobilized and dissected and vessel loops are passed around the lobar branches to provide distal control.
Right pulmonary artery segment in this area is good and normal in quality.
Then we start mobilizing the proximal portion of the right pulmonary artery, elevating it off the ascending aorta.
You can see the evidence of the previous patching.
Another vessel loop passed around the proximal right pulmonary artery.
We sized the proximal and distal portion.
Here is the left innominate vein is being mobilized to provide access to the distal ascending aorta in anticipation of the need for cardiopulmonary bypass.
We prepared a 12 millimeter ringed Gore-Tex graft.
And we just clamped the right pulmonary artery first, which appeared to have been tolerated by the patient.
So a proximal and distal control was provided with esargil clamps distally on the lobar branches and this vascular clamp on the proximal right pulmonary artery at its origin.
And you can see the internal diameter is way different from what it looks from outside.
Stay sutures are placed on the distal pulmonary artery.
Using a running 6-0 proline suture,
The distal anastomosis was reconstructed between the gothic graft and the distal right pulmonary artery at the takeoff of the lobar branches.
As we mentioned previously, the graft here has to be oversized to provide the longer re-intervention-free time.
All distal clamps were removed as well as the vessel loops and the anastomosis is tested.
Then the graft is tailored, marked and prepared for the proximal anastomosis to the main pulmonary artery.
We removed all the scarred proximal right pulmonary artery and all the patch materials.
And as we mentioned previously, at this stage, we did not think repeat patching will be of any benefit.
Also having the right pulmonary artery in an anterior location facilitated performing the procedure without any hemodynamic disturbance.
The proximal anastomosis is reconstructed using a running 5-opaline suture.
Then all clamps are removed and the anastomosis is de-aired.
This is the final appearance of the graft and it's lying in a good position with distal right pulmonary artery filled nicely.
The graft is oversized on purpose and once echocardiogram confirmed good flow in the distal branches,
we proceeded with reconstruction of the anterior pericardium and routine chest closure in the standard fashion.
